data.content:
Here are **three evaluation-phase performance issues** in this project:

---

### 1. Deep Import Chain (`level1 → level2 → level3`)

The `.csproj` imports `level1.props`, which imports `level2.props`, which imports `level3.props`. Each import adds an evaluation pass — MSBuild must locate, parse, and evaluate every file in the chain. Deep nesting (here 3 levels deep, but this pattern scales poorly) increases evaluation time and makes the project harder to reason about. Flatten imports where possible.

### 2. Overly Broad Glob Pattern (`**\*.*`)

```xml
<AdditionalFiles Include="**\*.*" Exclude="**\bin\**;**\obj\**" />
```

This glob walks the **entire directory tree** during evaluation. It will scan into `.git/`, `node_modules/`, and any other large subdirectory. This is evaluated at project-load time — before any build targets run — so it slows every IDE load, restore, and build invocation. Fix by restricting to specific extensions or adding exclusions via `DefaultItemExcludes`:

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <DefaultItemExcludes>$(DefaultItemExcludes);node_modules\**;.git\**</DefaultItemExcludes>
</PropertyGroup>
<AdditionalFiles Include="**\*.txt" />
```

### 3. File I/O Property Function During Evaluation

```xml
<BuildNotes>$([System.IO.File]::ReadAllText('build-notes.txt'))</BuildNotes>
```

Property functions execute **during evaluation**, not during build execution. `File::ReadAllText` is disk I/O that runs every time the project is evaluated (IDE load, restore, build). This is expensive and blocks evaluation. Move this logic into a custom target with `Inputs/Outputs` so it only runs during execution and only when the file changes.

---

**Key concept:** MSBuild has two phases — *evaluation* (parses XML, expands properties, resolves imports, expands globs) and *execution* (runs targets/tasks). All three issues above add overhead to the evaluation phase, which runs on every project load.
